walkthrough.title: Choose a Map
walkthrough.text: Let's get Power Grid set up. The board is double-sided with Germany on one side and the USA on the other. For your first game, Germany is recommended. Place the board in the center of the table.

walkthrough.title: Select Play Areas
walkthrough.text: The map is divided into six color-coded regions with seven cities each. Based on your player count, you will use only some of these regions. With two or three players, choose three adjacent connected regions. With four players, choose four. With five or six players, choose five. All unchosen regions are off limits for the entire game.

walkthrough.title: Fill the Resource Market
walkthrough.text: Now stock the resource market on the board. Place coal tokens on spaces one through eight, oil tokens on spaces three through eight, garbage tokens on spaces six through eight, and uranium tokens on spaces fourteen through sixteen. The remaining resource tokens go into a supply pile near the board.

walkthrough.title: Prepare Starting Money
walkthrough.text: Sort the money into denominations of one, five, ten, and fifty Elektro. This is the bank. Each player takes fifty Elektro as their starting cash. Give each player a payment summary card so they know how much income each city generates.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Power Plant Market
walkthrough.text: Find all the power plant cards. Separate the plug-back plants numbered three through fifteen from the socket-back plants. Shuffle the plug-back plants and reveal eight of them. Sort all eight in ascending order by their number. The four cheapest go in the top row, called the current market. The next four go in the bottom row, called the future market.

walkthrough.title: Remove Plants by Player Count
walkthrough.text: Depending on your player count, some plants get removed from the game. With two players, remove one plug-back and five socket-back plants face down. With three players, remove two plug-back and six socket-back plants. With four players, remove one plug-back and three socket-back plants. With five or six players, remove nothing.

walkthrough.title: Build the Draw Stack
walkthrough.text: Set the Step 3 card aside for now. Shuffle all remaining socket-back plants together with the leftover plug-back plants. Take one extra plug-back plant and set it face down on top of this shuffled pile. Place the Step 3 card face down at the bottom of the entire stack. This draw stack goes next to the market.

walkthrough.title: Place Resource Refill Card
walkthrough.text: Find the resource refill summary card that matches your player count. Place it near the board so everyone can reference it. This card tells you how many resources of each type get added to the market at the end of every round.

walkthrough.title: Player Setup
walkthrough.text: Each player takes twenty-two houses of one color. Place one house on space zero of the connected-cities track on the board. The rest of your houses are your supply for building into cities later.

walkthrough.title: Determine Initial Player Order
walkthrough.text: Randomly determine the starting player order. Place one house from each player on the player order track at random. This order will change at the start of every round based on who has the most cities connected.

walkthrough.title: Place Game Markers
walkthrough.text: Place the auction hammer and discount token near the plant market. Put the Step 2 barrier and Game End barrier near the connected-cities track. These markers trigger important game changes when players reach certain city thresholds.

walkthrough.title: Round One Rule
walkthrough.text: One critical rule for round one: every player must buy exactly one power plant. This is the only round where buying is mandatory. After round one, buying is always optional. Setup is complete. Let's learn how the five phases of each round work.

walkthrough.title: The Five Phases
walkthrough.text: Every round of Power Grid follows five phases in strict order. Phase 1: Determine Player Order. Phase 2: Auction Power Plants. Phase 3: Buy Resources. Phase 4: Build Houses on the map. Phase 5: Bureaucracy, where you run your plants and earn income. Let's walk through each one.

walkthrough.title: Phase 1: Determine Player Order
walkthrough.text: At the start of each round, update the player order. The player with the most connected cities goes first. If there is a tie, the player with the highest-numbered power plant among the tied players goes first. This order matters because the leader goes first in auctions but last in buying resources and building cities.

walkthrough.title: Phase 2: Auction Power Plants
walkthrough.text: The first player in order starts the auction. They may choose any plant from the current market of four and set a starting bid equal to or higher than the plant's number. Bidding goes clockwise. Each player must raise or pass. Passing removes you from that auction permanently. When only one bidder remains, they pay and take the plant.

walkthrough.title: Auction Details
walkthrough.text: Each player can buy at most one plant per round. After someone buys a plant, draw a replacement from the stack and re-sort both market rows. The discount token goes on the cheapest current market plant at the start of this phase, lowering its minimum bid to one Elektro. A player may also pass entirely, choosing not to bid at all this round.

walkthrough.title: Plant Ownership Rules
walkthrough.text: You can own a maximum of three power plants at any time. If you buy a fourth, you must scrap one of your existing plants immediately. You cannot scrap the one you just bought. Any resources on a scrapped plant move to your other plants if they have storage capacity. Excess resources return to the supply, not the resource market.

walkthrough.title: Phase 3: Buy Resources
walkthrough.text: In reverse player order, each player buys resources from the market. The player in last position buys first, giving trailing players an advantage. You can only buy resources that your plants can legally store. Each plant stores up to twice its required fuel amount. Pay the price printed on the market space for each resource you take.

walkthrough.title: Resource Pricing
walkthrough.text: The resource market works on supply and demand. As resources are bought, the remaining ones sit on more expensive spaces. Early buyers get cheap resources, and later buyers pay more. This means the more popular a fuel type is, the more expensive it becomes. Diversifying your fuel types helps you avoid price spikes.

walkthrough.title: Phase 4: Build Houses
walkthrough.text: Again in reverse player order, players build houses into cities on the map. Building costs two things: the connection cost printed between cities and the city slot cost. In Step 1, only the cheapest slot in each city is available, costing ten Elektro. In Step 2, the second slot opens at fifteen Elektro. In Step 3, the third slot opens at twenty Elektro.

walkthrough.title: Building Strategy
walkthrough.text: You must build from your existing network. Your first city can be anywhere in the chosen regions, but every subsequent city must connect to a city you already occupy. The connection cost between adjacent cities is printed on the routes between them. Plan your network to minimize connection costs.

walkthrough.title: Phase 5: Bureaucracy
walkthrough.text: Now you run your power plants. Choose which plants to operate and pay their exact fuel costs. Each plant powers a certain number of cities. You earn income based on how many cities you can actually power, not just how many you are connected to. Check the payment summary card for income amounts.

walkthrough.title: Bureaucracy: Resource Refill
walkthrough.text: After income, refill the resource market according to the refill card for your player count and current game step. Resources are placed on the cheapest available market spaces. This represents new fuel entering the economy. Different fuel types refill at different rates, which affects long-term pricing.

walkthrough.title: Bureaucracy: Plant Market Update
walkthrough.text: After refilling resources, update the power plant market. In Step 1, remove the highest-numbered plant from the future market, place it under the draw stack, and draw a replacement. In Step 2, remove the lowest-numbered plant from the current market entirely and draw a replacement. These rules keep the market fresh.

walkthrough.title: The Step System
walkthrough.text: Power Grid has three steps that change the rules as the game progresses. Step 2 triggers when any player connects a threshold number of cities, which is seven for two to five players or six for six players. Step 2 opens the second city slot and changes market and refill rules. Step 3 triggers when the Step 3 card is drawn from the plant stack.

walkthrough.title: Step 2 Changes
walkthrough.text: When Step 2 begins, remove the lowest-numbered plant from the current market. Now players can build in the second slot of each city for fifteen Elektro plus connection cost. The market update rules also change. Step 2 is a major transition that speeds up the game and opens expansion opportunities.

walkthrough.title: Step 3 Changes
walkthrough.text: Step 3 triggers when the Step 3 card comes out of the draw stack. Remove the Step 3 card and the lowest-numbered plant from the current market. Shuffle the remaining draw stack. Now the third city slot opens at twenty Elektro. The future market merges with the current market for drawing purposes. The endgame is approaching.

walkthrough.title: Turn Order Economy
walkthrough.text: Player order creates a fascinating tension in Power Grid. The leader in cities goes first in auctions, which gives them first pick of plants. But they go last in buying resources and building cities, meaning they pay more for fuel and get blocked on the map. Sometimes it is advantageous to stay in second or third place to get better resource and building access.

walkthrough.title: Power Plant Types
walkthrough.text: There are several types of power plants. Coal, oil, garbage, and uranium plants each use one specific fuel. Hybrid plants use any combination of coal and oil totaling their required amount. Ecological plants use no fuel at all but tend to power fewer cities. Each plant shows its fuel type, fuel requirement, and cities powered.

walkthrough.title: Storage Rules
walkthrough.text: Each power plant can store up to twice its required fuel. A plant that needs two coal to run can hold four coal. Hybrid plants can store any mix of coal and oil up to twice their total requirement. Ecological plants store nothing. Planning your storage is essential for managing fuel costs across rounds.

walkthrough.title: Game End Trigger
walkthrough.text: The game end triggers when any player connects a certain number of cities during Phase 4. The threshold depends on player count: eighteen cities for two players, seventeen for three or four players, fifteen for five players, and fourteen for six players. Once triggered, finish the current round through Phase 5.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: After the final Bureaucracy phase, each player runs their plants one last time. The winner is the player who powers the most cities in this final round. Not connected cities, but actually powered cities. You need the fuel and the plant capacity. If there is a tie, the player with the most Elektro remaining wins. If still tied, the most connected cities breaks it.

bullets: P1: Determine Order → P2: Auction Plants → P3: Buy Resources
P4: Build Houses → P5: Bureaucracy (run plants, income, refill)
Phases repeat every round in strict order

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Power Grid
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first round of Power Grid! We will walk through a scripted first round with four example players on the Germany map using four adjacent regions. Each player has fifty Elektro and no cities yet. This is the only round where buying a plant is mandatory. Let's get started.

walkthrough.title: Phase 1: Initial Player Order
walkthrough.text: Since nobody has any cities yet, the starting order is random. Let's say the order is Player 1, Player 2, Player 3, Player 4. Remember, this order will update every round once people start building cities.

walkthrough.title: Phase 2: The Auction Begins
walkthrough.text: The current market shows four power plants. Player 1 goes first and must choose a plant from the current market to start an auction. They pick plant number seven, which runs on three oil and powers two cities. The minimum bid is seven Elektro, matching the plant's number. Player 1 bids seven.

walkthrough.title: Bidding in Action
walkthrough.text: Player 2 raises to eight Elektro. Player 3 looks at the plant and decides it is not worth more, so they pass and are out of this auction. Player 4 raises to nine. Player 1 raises to ten. Player 2 passes. Player 4 passes. Player 1 wins plant seven for ten Elektro and has forty left.

walkthrough.title: Why That Auction Mattered
walkthrough.text: Player 1 overpaid a little for plant seven. The minimum bid was only seven Elektro, but competition drove it to ten. Every Elektro spent on auctions is money you cannot spend on resources and cities. Sometimes letting an opponent win a plant cheaply is better than driving up the price for both of you.

walkthrough.title: Remaining Players Buy Plants
walkthrough.text: Players 2, 3, and 4 each buy a plant from the current market. A replacement is drawn and sorted after each purchase. Player 2 gets a coal plant. Player 3 gets an ecological plant that needs no fuel. Player 4 gets a hybrid coal or oil plant. Everyone now has exactly one power plant.

walkthrough.title: Phase 3: Buy Resources
walkthrough.text: Now we buy resources in reverse player order. Player 4 buys first. They need coal or oil for their hybrid plant. Coal on market space one costs just one Elektro per token, so Player 4 buys two coal cheaply. Player 3 has an ecological plant and needs no fuel. Player 2 buys coal. Player 1 buys oil.

walkthrough.title: Player 1's Mistake: Ignoring Fuel Prices
walkthrough.text: Player 1 bought an oil plant, but by the time they buy in Phase 3, the cheapest oil costs four Elektro per token because earlier buyers already grabbed the cheap resources. Player 1 needs three oil to run their plant, costing twelve Elektro. That is almost a third of their remaining money on fuel alone.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Plan for Reverse Buying Order
walkthrough.text: In Power Grid, the leader buys resources last and pays the most. If you are going to buy an expensive fuel type like oil, factor in the likelihood that resources will be pricier by the time you get to buy. Sometimes a cheaper coal plant that buys fuel at lower prices is better than a flashy oil plant with expensive fuel costs.

walkthrough.title: Phase 4: Build Cities
walkthrough.text: Again in reverse order, players build their first cities. Player 4 chooses a city in a central region with good connections to nearby cities. They pay ten Elektro for the city slot. Player 3, 2, and 1 each build one city. Since nobody is connected yet, there are no connection costs for the first city.

walkthrough.title: Phase 5: Run Plants and Earn Income
walkthrough.text: Each player runs their power plant by paying its fuel cost. Player 1 pays three oil, Player 2 pays coal, Player 4 pays coal. Player 3's ecological plant runs for free. Everyone powers one city and earns income for one powered city. Check the payment card: powering one city earns twenty-two Elektro.

walkthrough.title: Resource Refill
walkthrough.text: The resource market gets refilled according to the four-player refill card. Coal, oil, garbage, and uranium tokens are added to the cheapest available market spaces. Prices drop back down as fresh supply enters. Players take note of which fuels refilled a lot versus a little.

walkthrough.title: Player 4's Mistake: Overbidding Round Two
walkthrough.text: In round two, Player 4 gets excited about a powerful plant that powers four cities. They bid aggressively, winning the plant for twenty-five Elektro. But now they only have fifteen Elektro left. They cannot afford to buy fuel and build a second city this round. Their expensive plant sits unused.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Budget Across All Phases
walkthrough.text: A common beginner trap is spending too much on auctions and having nothing left for resources and cities. Before you bid on a plant, calculate how much you will need for fuel, and how much you want to spend on building cities this round. Keep a cash buffer. A great plant you cannot fuel or build around is worthless.

walkthrough.title: Player 2's Mistake: Building Without Power
walkthrough.text: Player 2 builds three cities in round three, excited to expand their network. But their coal plant only powers two cities. They earn income for only two powered cities, not three. The third city cost money to build but generates no income yet.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Only Build What You Can Power
walkthrough.text: Connected cities only earn income if you can actually power them. Building cities you cannot power wastes money in the short term. Always match your building pace to your power generation capacity. Expand your plant capacity first, then build to fill it.

walkthrough.title: Mid-Game: Step 2 Approaches
walkthrough.text: As players connect more cities, someone will hit the Step 2 threshold of seven connected cities. When this happens, the rules change. A second slot opens in each city, and the plant market adjusts. Players should plan for this transition and time their building to either trigger or delay Step 2 depending on their position.

walkthrough.title: The Endgame Push
walkthrough.text: In the final rounds, everything comes together. You need enough connected cities, enough plant capacity to power them, and enough fuel to run your plants. Work backward from the city threshold: how many cities do you need to connect, and how many can you power? That backward planning is the key to winning Power Grid.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations! You have walked through the core flow of Power Grid. You learned how auctions work, why reverse player order matters for resources and building, and the importance of matching your building pace to your power capacity. You watched players learn from three key mistakes: ignoring fuel prices, overbidding on auctions, and building cities they cannot power.

walkthrough.title: Before You Power Up
walkthrough.text:
Before you play a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for auction strategy, fuel management, and network planning.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to plant types, step triggers, and city income.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

summary.title: Before You Power Up
bullets: Check out General Tips → auction strategy, fuel management, network planning
Reference the Appendix → plant types, step triggers, income table
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Budget Before You Bid
walkthrough.text: Before every auction, do the math. How much do you need for fuel this round? How much will it cost to build the cities you want? Whatever is left after those essentials is your auction budget. Overspending on a flashy plant and starving your resources and construction budget is the most common beginner mistake in Power Grid.

walkthrough.title: Reverse Order Is Your Friend
walkthrough.text: Trailing players buy resources and build cities first, which means cheaper fuel and better city choices. Being in first place sounds great, but it means you pay more for everything except auctions. Sometimes it is strategically correct to stay slightly behind in connected cities to maintain better resource and building access.

walkthrough.title: Diversify Your Fuel
walkthrough.text: If all three of your plants burn coal, you are at the mercy of the coal market. When other players also buy coal, prices spike. Having plants that use different fuel types gives you flexibility and protects you from price wars. An ecological plant that needs no fuel is especially valuable as insurance.

walkthrough.title: Plan Your Network Shape
walkthrough.text: Before you build your first city, look at the map and plan a rough route for your network. Low-cost connections between cities save enormous amounts of Elektro over the course of the game. Avoid building into dead ends where future expansion is expensive. Think three or four cities ahead when choosing your starting location.

walkthrough.title: Match Building to Power Capacity
walkthrough.text: Never build more cities than you can power. Each unpowered city costs you money to build but generates zero income. Before expanding your network, make sure you have the plant capacity and fuel to power every connected city. Build up your power generation first, then expand.

walkthrough.title: Watch the Step Triggers
walkthrough.text: Step 2 and Step 3 change the game significantly. When Step 2 hits, second city slots open up and the market adjusts. When Step 3 hits, third slots open and the endgame begins. Be aware of when these transitions are coming and position yourself to benefit from them rather than being caught off guard.

walkthrough.title: The Final Round Calculation
walkthrough.text: Power Grid is won in the final round. The winner is not the player with the most cities or the most money, but the player who powers the most cities in the last Bureaucracy phase. Work backward from the endgame: how many cities do you need to connect, how many plants can you run, and do you have enough fuel? Start planning this calculation several rounds before the end.

walkthrough.title: Upgrade Plants at the Right Time
walkthrough.text: Early in the game, cheap plants that power one or two cities are fine. But by mid-game, you need plants that power three or four cities each. Do not get attached to your early plants. Upgrade aggressively in the middle of the game so your power capacity can keep up with your network growth. A well-timed plant upgrade is often the turning point of the game.

summary.title: Budget Before You Bid
bullets: Calculate fuel + city costs BEFORE auction begins
Auction budget = leftover after essentials
Overspending on plants = most common beginner mistake

summary.title: Reverse Order Is Your Friend
bullets: Trailing players get cheaper resources and first city picks
Leading = pay more for everything except auctions
Staying slightly behind can be strategically correct

bullets: All-coal plants → coal price spikes hurt you hard

Eco plants = no fuel cost, excellent insurance

summary.title: Watch the Step Triggers
bullets: Step 2: 2nd city slots open, market adjusts

Position yourself to benefit from transitions

summary.title: The Final Round Calculation
bullets: Winner = most cities POWERED in final Bureaucracy (not most cities/money)
Work backward: cities + plants + fuel needed to win
Start planning several rounds before the end

summary.title: Upgrade Plants at the Right Time
bullets: Early game: 1-2 city plants are fine
Mid-game: upgrade to 3-4 city plants aggressively
Well-timed upgrade = often the turning point of the game

walkthrough.title: Auction Manipulation
walkthrough.text: Advanced players use auctions as weapons. You can bid up a plant you do not want to force an opponent to overpay, then let them win it at an inflated price. You can also pass strategically in early rounds to avoid going first in order, preserving your reverse-order advantages for resources and building. Every bid sends a signal. Use that to your advantage.

walkthrough.title: Controlled Position Strategy
walkthrough.text: Being in second or third place is often more powerful than being in first. You get better resource prices, earlier building access, and you can time your surge for the final rounds. Advanced players intentionally hold back on connecting cities to maintain reverse-order benefits, then explode forward in the last two or three rounds when it matters most.

walkthrough.title: Resource Market Denial
walkthrough.text: If you know an opponent relies on oil, buying extra oil to stock up on your hybrid plant denies them cheap fuel. This is especially powerful when you buy first in reverse order. A player whose fuel costs jump from eight to sixteen Elektro per round is effectively losing eight Elektro of income. Resource denial can cripple opponents more effectively than any other strategy.

walkthrough.title: Network Blocking
walkthrough.text: In Step 1, each city only has one slot. By building in key junction cities, you can cut off opponents' expansion routes and force them into expensive detours. This is especially effective in tight map regions. Even spending a few extra Elektro on a strategically placed city is worthwhile if it blocks an opponent's cheapest expansion path.

walkthrough.title: Step Timing Control
walkthrough.text: Triggering Step 2 at the right moment can give you a huge advantage. If you are prepared for the transition and your opponents are not, the rule changes benefit you disproportionately. Conversely, delaying Step 2 by holding back on your seventh city can give you one more round of cheap building and resource access before the market shifts.

walkthrough.title: Plant Portfolio Optimization
walkthrough.text: The ideal plant portfolio by endgame is three plants that together can power enough cities to win, using fuel types that are cheap and well-stocked. An ecological plant removes fuel dependency for some of your cities. A high-capacity coal or garbage plant provides bulk power. A hybrid plant gives fuel flexibility. Plan your scrapping decisions to build toward this optimal portfolio.

walkthrough.title: Fuel Stockpiling
walkthrough.text: Plants can store up to twice their required fuel. Buying extra fuel when prices are low and storing it for future rounds is a powerful economic move. This is especially important before Step transitions when refill rates or market dynamics might change. A full fuel reserve means you can skip buying resources for a round and spend that money on cities instead.

walkthrough.title: Backward Planning
walkthrough.text: The best Power Grid players start planning backward from the endgame three or four rounds before the end. They calculate exactly how many cities they need to connect, which plants they need running, and how much fuel they must stockpile. Then they work backward round by round to determine exactly when to buy each plant, when to stock fuel, and when to build each city. This level of planning separates good players from great ones.

walkthrough.title: The Discount Token Edge
walkthrough.text: The discount token makes the cheapest current market plant cost only one Elektro minimum. Advanced players sometimes target the discounted plant specifically for its value. A small plant with a one Elektro minimum bid can be an incredible deal, especially if it powers two or three cities. Watch for discount opportunities that other players overlook.

walkthrough.title: Final Round Surge
walkthrough.text: The ultimate Power Grid move is the final round surge. You have been sitting quietly in second or third place, buying resources cheaply and building steadily. Then in the final round, you connect your remaining cities, run all three plants at full capacity, and power more cities than anyone expected. The leader, who went first all game and paid premium prices, suddenly finds themselves outpowered. That is Power Grid mastery.

summary.title: Auction Manipulation
bullets: Bid up plants you don't want to force opponent overpays
Pass strategically to preserve reverse-order advantages
Every bid sends a signal — use it as misinformation

summary.title: Controlled Position Strategy
bullets: 2nd-3rd place often > 1st place (cheaper resources, earlier builds)
Hold back cities to maintain reverse-order benefits
Surge forward in final 2-3 rounds when it matters

summary.title: Resource Market Denial
bullets: Buy extra fuel opponents need, especially with reverse-order advantage
Opponent fuel costs jumping 8→16E = crippling
Most powerful denial strategy in Power Grid

summary.title: Network Blocking
bullets: Build in junction cities to cut off opponent expansion
Step 1: only 1 slot per city = blocking is very effective
Worth spending extra to block opponent's cheapest path

summary.title: Step Timing Control
bullets: Trigger Step 2 when you're prepared and opponents aren't
Delay Step 2 by holding back on 7th city for 1 more cheap round
Step transitions are strategic weapons, not just rules

summary.title: Plant Portfolio Optimization
bullets: Ideal: 3 plants covering enough cities with diverse, cheap fuel
Eco plant = no fuel dependency for some cities
Plan scrapping decisions toward optimal endgame portfolio

summary.title: Fuel Stockpiling
bullets: Buy extra fuel when cheap → store at 2x capacity
Stockpile before Step transitions that change refill rates
Full reserve = skip resource buying → more money for cities

summary.title: Backward Planning
bullets: Start planning backward 3-4 rounds before endgame
Calculate: cities needed + plants to run + fuel to stockpile
Work backward round-by-round to schedule every action

summary.title: The Discount Token Edge
bullets: Discounted plant min bid = 1 Elektro (huge bargain potential)
Small plants at 1E are incredible deals
Watch for discount opportunities others overlook

summary.title: Final Round Surge
bullets: Sit in 2nd-3rd place all game with cheap resources/builds
Final round: connect remaining cities, run all plants at full capacity
Outpower the leader who paid premium prices all game = mastery

entries.definition: The currency of Power Grid. Used to buy plants, resources, and build cities. Starting cash is 50 Elektro per player. Serves as tie-break in final scoring.
Cards that generate power for cities. Each shows a minimum bid number, fuel type and amount, and cities powered. Players can own a maximum of three plants.
A power plant that burns coal (brown tokens) to power cities. Coal is the most abundant resource in the market but can get expensive when contested.
A power plant that burns oil (black tokens) to power cities. Oil starts at moderate availability and refills at a steady rate.
A power plant that burns garbage (yellow tokens) to power cities. Garbage starts scarce but becomes more available as the game progresses.
A power plant that uses uranium (red tokens) to power cities. Uranium is very scarce and expensive but powers many cities per token.
A power plant that can use any combination of coal and oil to meet its total fuel requirement. Storage holds coal and oil mixed up to 2x total requirement.
A power plant that uses no fuel. It powers cities for free each round but typically powers fewer cities than fuel-burning plants of similar market value.
The top row of four power plants available for auction. Only plants from this row can be chosen by the active player to start an auction.
The bottom row of four power plants visible but not yet available for auction. Plants move from future to current market as purchases and replacements occur.
Placed on the cheapest current market plant at the start of Phase 2. Reduces the minimum bid to 1 Elektro. If the discounted plant is not bought by phase end, it is removed from the game.
The price track on the board where coal, oil, garbage, and uranium tokens sit. Lower-numbered spaces are cheaper. As resources are bought, remaining ones sit on more expensive spaces.
Each plant stores up to 2x its required fuel amount. Hybrid plants store a combined coal/oil total up to 2x. Ecological plants store nothing.
A city where you have built a house. Connected cities form your network. You earn income only from cities you can actually power, not just connected ones.
The cost printed on routes between adjacent cities. Paid when building to expand your network. Lower connection costs make for more efficient expansion routes.
Each city has three building slots with increasing costs. Step 1: slot 1 costs 10 Elektro. Step 2 opens slot 2 at 15 Elektro. Step 3 opens slot 3 at 20 Elektro.
Rank players by most connected cities, then highest plant number for ties. The leader goes first in auctions but last in resource buying and building.
Players auction plants from the current market. Active player picks a plant and sets opening bid. Clockwise bidding: raise or pass. Max 1 purchase per round per player.
In reverse player order, buy fuel from the resource market. Pay printed market prices. Can only buy resources your plants can legally store.
In reverse player order, build houses into cities. Pay connection cost plus city slot cost. Must connect to existing network after first city.
Run power plants by paying fuel. Earn income based on cities powered. Refill resource market. Update plant market. Income table is on the payment summary card.
The opening stage of the game. Only the first (cheapest) slot in each city is available. The highest future market plant is cycled under the draw stack at end of each round.
Triggered when a player connects 7 cities (6 for 6 players). Opens the second city slot at 15 Elektro. The lowest current market plant is removed from the game each round.
Triggered when the Step 3 card is drawn from the plant stack. Opens the third city slot at 20 Elektro. Future market merges with current market. Remaining draw stack is reshuffled.
In Phases 3 and 4, the player in last position acts first. This gives trailing players cheaper resources and earlier building access, creating a natural catch-up mechanism.
A connected city for which you have a running power plant with fuel. Only powered cities earn income. The final winner is determined by most powered cities, not connected cities.
The number of connected cities that triggers the final round. 2 players: 18 cities. 3-4 players: 17. 5 players: 15. 6 players: 14. Finish the round through Bureaucracy.
Reference card showing income for each number of powered cities. Powers 0 cities = 10 Elektro, scaling up. Powers 20 cities = 150 Elektro. Essential reference during Bureaucracy.
Reference card specific to player count showing how many of each resource type are added to the market during Bureaucracy. Different refill rates for each step.
When buying a 4th plant, you must scrap one existing plant. Cannot scrap the just-purchased plant. Resources from scrapped plant transfer to other plants with capacity; excess returns to supply.
The subset of map regions used for the game based on player count. All chosen regions must be contiguous and connected. Unchosen regions are off-limits for the entire game.
Track on the board showing current player order. Updated at the start of each round based on connected cities and highest plant number for ties.
Required for 2-player games. A third color of 16 houses acts as the Trust, blocking cities and buying plants automatically. The Trust cannot trigger Step 2 or win the game.
Track on the board where each player marks their total connected cities. Used for determining player order and tracking Step 2 and Game End triggers.
Marker placed near the plant market. Used as a reference point during the auction phase.
